lesson.menu_title: Small Talk That Works -- Modal & Conditional Consolidation
lesson.menu_desc: Start and sustain casual conversations with native speakers -- consolidating modal verbs and the second conditional in social contexts -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 19 -- Small Talk That Works -- Modal & Conditional Consolidation (Review)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 19 -- Small Talk That Works (Consolidation)

listenings.text: Let me tell you about my morning. I was standing in line at a coffee shop, and the guy next to me had a bag from a bookstore I love. So I just said, no kidding, I was in that store yesterday. He smiled, and we started talking. Honestly, at first I didn't know what to say, so I used a lot of little words, you know, like, I mean, right? Those filler words are not a problem. They give you a second to think, and they tell the other person you are still with them. We talked about the weather, the neighborhood, a new bakery around the corner. Nothing deep. But by the time I had my coffee, it felt easy. That reminds me of something a friend told me once. She said small talk is not about the words. It is about the signal. You are saying, I am open, I am friendly, we could talk. And if I had stayed quiet that morning, I would have missed a really nice five minutes. So now, when I see a small opening, I take it.

People ask me all the time what they should talk about with a stranger. My answer is simple. Stay light. The safe topics almost always work: the weather, the place you are both in, weekend plans, a good restaurant, a show everyone is watching. You might want to avoid money, politics, and anything too personal at first. Here is the part that surprises people. You do not need a clever opener. You could just say something small and true. I couldn't help but notice your jacket. What brings you here today? Have you been to this place before? Then you listen, and you react. No kidding. That is so interesting. Tell me more. If I were nervous, I would remember one thing. The other person is probably a little nervous too. And speaking of nerves, a few filler words are your friends. They make you sound human. So keep it light, stay curious, and let the conversation breathe.

extra_audio.text: First, notice a small, safe opening, like a bag, a jacket, or the weather. Then, open with a light comment or question, such as I couldn't help but notice, or what brings you here. Next, listen closely and react warmly, saying things like no kidding, or that is interesting. After that, keep it going with a follow-up, using speaking of, or that reminds me. Finally, close in a friendly way, saying it was great talking to you.
